gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: Just got back from the front. Still have mud on my boots from burying three centurions outside Asculum. The war is going badly. The Italians fight like Romans because we trained them to fight like Romans. Turns out that was a mistake.

responses.playerDialogue: How bad? Give me numbers, not speeches.
If the war is going badly, someone in Rome is already figuring out how to profit from it.
What do the commanders say? Is there a plan to turn this around?
I'm sorry about your centurions. Good men?

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Tell me what happened out there.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Two legions routed in Samnium. Lupus dead with half his army at the Tolenus River. The Marsi hit us at dawn and we never recovered the line. Back in Rome, the senate debates who gets credit for victories that haven't happened. Meanwhile, every Italian town between here and Brundisium is armed and angry. We need bodies. Trained ones. I have the authority to issue military credentials. You want to make a difference, or you want to keep talking about it?

options.playerDialogue: Give me a posting. I'll fight.
What about Sulla? I hear he's been effective in Campania.
I'm more useful behind the lines. Supply, logistics, intelligence. Where do you need that kind of help?

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: Best men I served with. One of them, Sextus, survived the Cimbri. Fifteen years carrying a shield for Rome. Killed by an Italian who'd been his tentmate in Numidia. That's what this war is. Brothers killing brothers because the senate couldn't share a vote. But I didn't come back to mourn. I came back to find people who can actually help. The front needs officers. Rome needs someone watching the supply lines. Which are you?

options.playerDialogue: Put me on the front line. I didn't come this far to count grain sacks.
What about Sulla? I hear he's been effective in Campania.
The supply lines. A war is won with bread and iron before it's won with swords. Put me where the logistics break down.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Good. I'll write your commission as military tribune attached to the populares levies. You'll serve under Marian officers. The pay is irregular and the food is worse. But you'll have command authority over a century, and when this war is done, every man in Rome will know you stood on the line. The veterans will consider you one of their own. That's worth more than any senate endorsement.

options.playerDialogue: I'll serve. But I want to know who I'm really fighting for — Rome or Marius.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Sulla. Yes. He's competent. More than competent — he's winning his sector while the rest of us bleed. Took Stabiae, pushed the Samnites back from Pompeii. The men worship him because he wins and he shares the loot. That's the Marian model, ironically. Sulla learned it from the old man and now he's better at it. Don't repeat that. The point is, when this war ends, Sulla will have an army that loves him and a reputation the senate can't ignore. That should worry everyone.

options.playerDialogue: Noted. Now — what can I do to help on the Marian side?

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Smart. The supply chain from Ostia to the southern front is a disaster. Grain shipments delayed, equipment misrouted, half the quartermasters are skimming. I'll commission you as a staff officer — you'll have access to the logistics network and the authority to audit the supply chain. Less glory, more impact. The men who keep an army fed win more battles than the men who swing swords.

options.playerDialogue: I'll take the commission. Where do I start?

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: Dawn. Pack light. Your century is assembling at the Porta Capena. They're raw but they'll learn — the Italians will teach them fast enough. You'll get your first taste of the line within a week. When you come back, you'll be a different man. Every soldier is.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: Fair question. The honest answer is both. Right now they're the same thing. Marius built this army, and this army is the only thing standing between Rome and the Italians. When the war ends, maybe that changes. But today? You're fighting for the men beside you. That's all any soldier ever fights for.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: The depot at Capua. It's the bottleneck. Fix that and you fix half the southern front's problems. I'll send word ahead that you're coming. Don't let the quartermasters stall you — half of them are on the take. Use your authority. That's what it's for.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Your choice. The war will end with or without you. But the men who didn't serve will find the peace harder to navigate. Military credibility matters in Rome. Always has. Without it, you're just another civilian with opinions.

deltas.reason: Volunteered for front-line service under Marian command
Chose combat when safer options were available
Supporting the Marian war effort from the supply lines
Chose the practical work of logistics over front-line glory
